FT_IC1.evidence: "We analyzed prospective cohort data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study (n = 9,208)" recruited from 21 U.S. sites; ABCD 4.0 NDA release used.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis of ABCD data; logistic regression models estimating associations between baseline screen time and OCD at 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Adolescent Health (2023); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports per-modality adjusted ORs for phone/SM-specific variables paired with OCD (individual-trait mental health outcome). Social media aOR 1.01 (0.81-1.27), texting aOR 1.13 (0.94-1.36) / 1.20 (1.00-1.44), video chat aOR 0.99 (0.80-1.22) in Models 2/3. These are phone/SM-specific variable labels with numeric estimates paired with OCD (psychiatric diagnosis). Step 1 satisfied.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios with 95% CIs reported in Table 3 for all screen time modalities vs OCD at 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential logistic regression analyses conducted; numeric estimates (AOR, 95% CI, p-values) reported throughout Table 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including phone/SM variables) serves as the IV; OCD is the DV. EC7 requires SM to be DV-only. Here SM/phone is IV, so EC7 does not apply.
notes: Social media and texting were not statistically significant predictors of OCD in adjusted models, but video games and watching videos were. All six screen modalities (including social media, texting, video chat) have their own numeric estimates in Table 3, satisfying IC2 Step 1 for phone/SM-specific variables.

FT_IC1.evidence: "We analyzed cross-sectional data from 10,280 early adolescents aged 10-14 (48.8% female) in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (Year 2, 2018-2020)"; 21 U.S. research sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD Year 2 data; modified Poisson regression estimating associations between bedtime screen behaviors and sleep outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Sleep Health (2023); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports adjusted risk ratios for phone/SM-specific variables paired with sleep outcomes (individual-trait health outcome). "Talk on the phone or text" ARR 1.16 (1.09-1.23) for trouble falling/staying asleep; "Spend time online on social media" ARR 1.16 (1.10-1.23) for trouble falling/staying asleep and ARR 1.06 (1.01-1.11) for sleep disturbance; "Leave the ringer on" ARR 1.23 (1.03-1.46) vs turning phone off. These are phone/SM-specific labels with their own numeric estimates paired with sleep (individual-trait health). Step 1 satisfied.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted risk ratios with 95% CIs and p-values reported in Table 3 for all bedtime screen behaviors vs sleep outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses conducted; numeric estimates (ARR, 95% CI, p-values) reported throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 requires longitudinal design. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Phone/SM variables (texting, social media, phone ringer behavior) are exposures (IV) and sleep outcomes are DVs. Multiple phone-specific bedtime behaviors have separate numeric estimates in Table 3.

FT_IC1.evidence: "We analyzed prospective data from the ABCD Study, a longitudinal study of brain development and adolescent health in 11,875 children recruited from 21 sites around the United States"; ABCD 3.0 NDA release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort analysis of ABCD data; modified Poisson regression estimating associations between baseline screen time modalities and conduct disorder / oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry (2023); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Tables 2 and 3 report per-modality prevalence ratios for phone/SM-specific variables paired with conduct disorder and ODD (individual-trait psychiatric outcomes). Social media PR 1.62 (1.39-1.87) for conduct disorder; texting PR 1.19 (1.07-1.33) for ODD; video chat PR 1.21 (1.06-1.37) for ODD. These are phone/SM-specific variable labels with their own numeric estimates paired with psychiatric diagnoses. Step 1 satisfied.

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted prevalence ratios (PR) with 95% CIs reported in Tables 2 and 3 for all screen time modalities vs conduct disorder and ODD at 1-year follow-up.

FT_IC4.evidence: Modified Poisson regression analyses conducted; numeric estimates (PR, 95% CI, p-values) reported throughout Tables 2-4.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including social media, texting, video chat) serves as IV; conduct disorder and ODD are DVs. EC7 requires SM to be DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis. Here SM/phone is IV throughout, so EC7 does not apply.
notes: Social media had the strongest association with conduct disorder (PR 1.62). All six screen modalities have separate numeric estimates. Prospective design with 1-year follow-up from ABCD baseline (ages 9-10) to follow-up (ages 10-11).
